pet.name: Saw
pet.special_needs: Needs more oxygen than other fishes..
pet.characteristics: High-oxygen fish typically include active species such as goldfish, plecos, or fast-swimming cyprinids that require robust aeration and water circulation. Care involves maintaining dissolved oxygen levels through air stones, powerheads, or surface agitation; regular water changes (25–50% weekly) to prevent oxygen depletion; cooler water temperatures when possible, as cold water holds more oxygen; and avoiding overcrowding. Monitor for gasping at the surface, lethargy, or rapid gill movement—signs of hypoxia. Diet should be species-appropriate (flakes, pellets, or vegetables depending on type) offered once or twice daily in amounts consumed within 2–3 minutes. Tank size should accommodate activity level and bioload. Enrichment includes plants, hiding spots, and open swimming space. Handling is generally avoided; observe behavior and water parameters closely.
pet.species_profile_summary: Lifespan varies widely by specific species (3–10 years typical for common high-oxygen fish in captive care); success depends on consistent aeration, water quality, and appropriate tank conditions.

age_context.summary: Lifespan varies widely by specific species (3–10 years typical for common high-oxygen fish in captive care); success depends on consistent aeration, water quality, and appropriate tank conditions.

goal: Create a complete pet care plan with daily, weekly, monthly tasks, reminders, and safe condition-aware guidance.

retrieved_passages.title: Medication Routine and Safety
retrieved_passages.content: Medication-related tasks in a home routine should focus on reminders, documentation, and observation, not dosage changes. A planning assistant can remind the owner to give medication exactly as already prescribed and to log whether the dose was completed.

retrieved_passages.title: Senior Pet Support
retrieved_passages.content: A senior pet plan should prioritize consistency over intensity. When time is limited, essential care such as feeding, hydration, medication reminders, and mobility support should be scheduled before optional enrichment.

retrieved_passages.title: Enrichment and Daily Monitoring
retrieved_passages.content: Observation tasks are useful for special-needs pets because they create a record of appetite, water intake, energy, and bathroom habits. These tasks should be short, specific, and easy to complete consistently.

retrieved_passages.title: Kidney Diet and Hydration Support
retrieved_passages.content: Owners should follow the veterinary feeding plan already prescribed and avoid changing food or supplement amounts without veterinary guidance. If a pet refuses multiple meals or shows sudden decline, the safe action is to contact a veterinarian.

retrieved_passages.title: Senior Pet Support
retrieved_passages.content: Older pets may benefit from routines that include medication reminders, slow feeding, and extra time for transitions. Owners should watch for changes in gait, confusion, appetite, and bathroom habits and report major changes to a veterinarian.

raw_recommendations.name: Monitor water oxygen levels and surface behavior
raw_recommendations.notes: Observe Saw for gasping at surface, rapid gill movement, or lethargy—signs of low dissolved oxygen. Check that aeration equipment (air stone, powerhead) is functioning properly.
raw_recommendations.rationale: High-oxygen fish require consistent monitoring to detect hypoxia early. Daily observation creates a record of behavior and water conditions.

raw_recommendations.name: Feed Saw species-appropriate food
raw_recommendations.notes: Offer appropriate food (flakes, pellets, or vegetables depending on species) in amounts Saw consumes within 2–3 minutes. Remove uneaten food to maintain water quality.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Consistent feeding supports energy and health. Portion control prevents overfeeding, which degrades water quality and reduces oxygen availability.

raw_recommendations.name: Check aeration and water circulation equipment
raw_recommendations.notes: Verify air stone, powerhead, or surface agitation is running. Listen for normal operation and check for blockages or reduced flow.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Robust aeration is essential for high-oxygen fish. Daily checks prevent equipment failure that could cause rapid oxygen depletion.

raw_recommendations.name: Perform 25–50% water change
raw_recommendations.notes: Change 25–50% of tank water weekly to prevent oxygen depletion and maintain water quality. Use dechlorinated water at appropriate temperature.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Regular water changes are critical for high-oxygen fish to remove waste, replenish dissolved oxygen, and maintain stable conditions.

raw_recommendations.name: Monitor water temperature
raw_recommendations.notes: Check tank temperature daily. Cooler water holds more dissolved oxygen; maintain temperature appropriate for Saw's species when possible.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Temperature directly affects oxygen solubility. Monitoring helps maintain optimal conditions for a high-oxygen fish.

raw_recommendations.name: Inspect tank for overcrowding and bioload
raw_recommendations.notes: Ensure tank is not overcrowded and bioload is manageable. Overcrowding reduces oxygen availability and increases waste.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Tank stocking and bioload directly impact dissolved oxygen levels. Weekly inspection prevents conditions that lead to hypoxia.

raw_recommendations.name: Verify hiding spots and open swimming space
raw_recommendations.notes: Confirm plants, hiding spots, and open swimming areas are in place and accessible. These support natural behavior and reduce stress.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Enrichment reduces stress and supports overall health. Consistent environment aids monitoring and observation.

raw_recommendations.name: Document Saw's appetite, energy, and behavior
raw_recommendations.notes: Keep brief notes on feeding response, activity level, and any changes in behavior. Report major changes to a veterinarian.
raw_recommendations.rationale: Observation records for special-needs pets create a baseline and help detect early signs of decline or illness.
